title: Measurement circuit ansatz: Naimark versus quantum neural-network measurements
authors: Sung Won Yun, Thi Ha Kyaw, Joonwoo Bae
abstract: In this work, we present constructions of quantum circuits to implement general measurements on quantum hardware. Firstly, we investigate a quantum circuit ansatz by following the Naimark extension with a universal set of gates, such as controlled-NOT and single-qubit gates; we call it a Naimark quantum measurement. We present a circuit ansatz framed by the Naimark extension, leaving single-qubit gates with parameters, and apply a classical optimizer to determine their parameters to approximate a desired quantum measurement. Secondly, we relax the Naimark measurement with quantum neural-network (QNN) circuits, employing parameterized quantum circuits. We present hybrid Naimark-QNN measurements by incorporating QNN circuits into Naimark measurements. Thirdly, we also consider fully QNN measurements with shallow parameterized circuits. Then, we compare the constructed measurement circuits, Naimark, hybrid Naimark-QNN, and fully QNN measurements, for strategies of state discrimination, such as minimum-error and maximum-confidence measurements. We demonstrate that QNN circuits can efficiently and effectively achieve near-optimal quantum measurements with fewer training iterations.
summary: The paper develops methods to build quantum circuits that perform general measurements (POVMs) by comparing the traditional Naimark extension against modern Quantum Neural Network (QNN) ansatzes. It demonstrates that while Naimark circuits are theoretically sound, QNN-based measurements offer superior optimization efficiency and hardware compatibility for state discrimination tasks.
main_problem: The primary goal is constructing quantum circuits (ansatze) capable of implementing general Positive-Operator-Valued Measures (POVMs) for tasks like state discrimination.
main_result: Quantum Neural Network (QNN) measurements are shown to be more efficient and effective than traditional Naimark circuit constructions, especially on near-term hardware, although they may not reach the theoretical optimum.
method: The work compares three measurement approaches: standard Naimark extensions using universal gates, hybrid Naimark-QNN circuits, and fully QNN measurements. Optimization is performed classically to determine parameters for state discrimination tasks (ME/MC).
model_or_system: The model involves quantum systems requiring the realization of $l$-outcome POVMs via ancillary qubits. The comparison focuses on circuit complexity, specifically counting CNOT gates and assessing optimization convergence.
key_observables: Guessing probability ($p_{	ext{guess}}$) for ME discrimination, Maximum Confidence (MC) values, and the required number of CNOT gates to implement the measurement circuit.
important_parameters: The number of desired outcomes ($l$), the minimum required ancillary qubits ($\lceil \log_2 l ceil$), and the optimization cutoff parameter ($\delta_c$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that current quantum hardware favors shallow-depth circuits, which biases the comparison toward QNN approaches over deep Naimark structures.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the construction of POVMs using ancillary qubits and binary modules; comparisons show convergence plots for different measurement types on simulated/real hardware; and circuit diagrams detail block-encoding and unitary transformations.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops from the Naimark extension formalism to constructing $l$-outcome POVM circuits. It then introduces QNN replacements (hybrid and full) to mitigate optimization difficulties inherent in the original structure, culminating in performance comparisons for state discrimination tasks.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses resource estimation and hardware feasibility for quantum measurement protocols, which is crucial for any practical application of quantum computation or sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: A general measurement can be implemented in a quantum circuit via the Naimark extension. A circuit USA over a system and ancilla qubits is performed and an outcome on ancilla qubits, say i (yellow), implements a general operation described by a Kraus operator Ki, where Ki =A ⟨i|USA.

FIG. 2: The construction of a measurement circuit is detailed: l-outcome POVM elements for measurements on 1 + log2 el qubits are shown by following the Naimark theorem [13, 23] where el = 2⌈log2 l⌉is the nearest power of 2 not smaller than l. (a) Two-outcome measurements can be prepared after a binary module BSA1 in Eq. (7). (b) After the first BSA2 and CX(01), a three-outcome measurement is realized. To add a POVM element, gate BSA2 is applied, after which four-outcome measurement is achieved. (c) Similarly, a five-outcome measurement is realized after the first BSA3 and CX(001). Then, a POVM can be added after two gates BSA3 and CX. Hence, eight-outcome measurements are realized. (d) A...

FIG. 3: Multi-qubit POVM extended from the single-qubit case. The single-qubit unitaries U and V are replaced with multi-qubit gates. The two single-qubit RY rotations can be interpreted to uniformly controlled rotation gates applied to the given single-qubit state, and thus generalized to uniformly controlled rotations on the prepared multi-qubit state. The CX(x) gates remains unchanged. The overall construction principles of the POVM are preserved. See Fig.2 for the definitions of el and β.

FIG. 5: A hybrid Naimark-QNN measurement for two- qubit states is shown. In a Naimark circuit in Fig. 3, and multi-qubit unitaries on a system are replaced by PQCs. Note that a Naimark and a hybrid Naimark- QNN measurements share the same circuit structure: CNOT gates to include ancillary qubits are placed in the same way.

FIG. 7: A fully QNN quantum measurement circuit USA is shown, where each layer is constructed by HEA. Pa- rameters of single-qubit gates in HEA are optimized. Multiple layers may enhance QNN measurements by increasing expressibility, approximating ideal measure- ments.
